title: Medicare Out-of-Pocket Costs: Your Guide to Copays, Deductibles, and Maximizing Savings
rawContent:
What Are Medicare Out-of-Pocket Costs?

Most people enrolling in Medicare are surprised to learn that the program comes with a variety of costs beyond the monthly premium. Medicare out-of-pocket costs include four primary types of medicare cost sharing: premiums, deductibles, copays, and coinsurance. Each works differently, and together they shape how much you actually pay for your healthcare throughout the year.

A premium is the monthly amount you pay to have coverage, regardless of whether you use any services. A deductible is what you pay before your plan starts sharing costs. A copay is a fixed dollar amount, say, $20 for a doctor visit. Coinsurance is a percentage you owe after meeting your deductible, such as 20% of an outpatient procedure. These terms are explained in detail at deductible, copay, coinsurance, and premium.

According to Kaiser Family Foundation (KFF) research, the average Medicare beneficiary spends approximately $6,500 per year out of pocket on healthcare. Beneficiaries with multiple chronic conditions or serious illnesses often spend significantly more. For those living on fixed incomes, unexpected medical bills rank among the most pressing financial stressors.

Your total costs depend heavily on which parts of Medicare you have enrolled in, Parts A, B, C, and D each carry their own cost structures. Understanding how they interact is the first step toward protecting your finances.

Medicare Part A Costs: What You Pay for Hospital and Inpatient Care

Medicare Part A covers inpatient hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care, and certain home health services. While most people do not pay a premium for Part A, the cost-sharing structure can be significant once you are admitted to the hospital.

In 2026, the Part A inpatient hospital deductible is $1,736 per benefit period (CMS, 2026). One critical detail many beneficiaries miss: this deductible resets per benefit period, not per calendar year. If you are discharged and readmitted after 60 days, you owe it again. Two hospitalizations in the same year could mean $3,472 in deductible costs alone.

The coinsurance structure adds up quickly for extended stays:

  • Days 1–60: $0 coinsurance (the deductible covers this period)

  • Days 61–90: $434 per day in 2026

  • Lifetime reserve days: $868 per day in 2026

A 75-day hospital stay, for example, would generate over $6,500 in coinsurance charges beyond the initial deductible, illustrating how original medicare expenses for hospital stays can become unpredictable. Skilled nursing facility stays add another layer: after day 20, you pay $217 per day in SNF coinsurance through day 100, and Medicare coverage ends entirely after day 100.

Medicare Part B Costs: Premiums, Deductibles, and Coinsurance for Medical Services

Part B covers outpatient medical services, doctor visits, preventive care, and medically necessary treatments. In 2026, the standard Part B monthly premium is $202.90, and the annual deductible is $283 (CMS, 2026). Once you meet the deductible, Medicare typically pays 80% of approved costs, and you pay the remaining 20% coinsurance.

Here is where the risk becomes real. That 20% has no cap under Original Medicare. A cancer treatment regimen costing $100,000 would leave you personally responsible for $20,000, with no ceiling on what you could owe. This makes Part B coinsurance one of the most significant gaps in Original Medicare coverage.

Higher-income beneficiaries also face IRMAA surcharges on top of the standard premium. In 2026, Part B premiums range from $202.90 up to $689.90 per month depending on income. This is covered in greater detail in the IRMAA section below.

As KFF's Juliette Cubanski, Deputy Director of the Program on Medicare Policy, has noted: "Original Medicare has no out-of-pocket maximum, which is why supplemental coverage like Medigap or Medicare Advantage is so important for financial protection against catastrophic costs." This is the central financial vulnerability every Medicare enrollee should understand before they need expensive care.

Medicare Part D Costs: What You Pay for Prescription Drug Coverage

Part D provides prescription drug coverage, either through a standalone plan or bundled within a Medicare Advantage plan. In 2026, the average Part D premium is $34. 50 per month, and the maximum deductible is $615 meaning some plans require you to pay full drug costs out of pocket before coverage begins. You can review current Part D costs in detail before choosing a plan.

Part D benefits move through distinct phases during the year:

  • Deductible phase: You pay full cost until you meet your plan's deductible (up to $615).

  • Initial coverage phase: Your plan begins sharing costs with you.

  • Catastrophic coverage phase: Once your true out-of-pocket drug spending reaches $2,100 in 2026, you pay $0 for covered prescriptions for the rest of the year.

That $2,100 catastrophic threshold, a significant benefit introduced under the Inflation Reduction Act, provides a hard stop on drug costs for beneficiaries managing expensive conditions like cancer or rheumatoid arthritis. Higher-income enrollees also pay IRMAA surcharges on Part D premiums, ranging from an additional $14. 50 to $91. 00 per month in 2026. And if you delay Part D enrollment without creditable coverage, a late enrollment penalty is calculated using the base premium of $38.

99 and added permanently to your monthly cost.

Does Original Medicare Have an Out-of-Pocket Maximum? Understanding the Coverage Gap

Original Medicare, Parts A and B combined, has no annual out-of-pocket maximum. The answer is straightforward: This surprises many new enrollees who assume that Medicare works like typical employer insurance. It does not. Your financial exposure under Original Medicare is theoretically unlimited.

Consider a real-world example. A beneficiary diagnosed with cancer who undergoes multiple rounds of chemotherapy could face tens of thousands of dollars in 20% Part B coinsurance with no safety net. Add a related inpatient stay and the costs compound further, all without any cap kicking in.

As David Lipschutz, Associate Director and Senior Policy Attorney at the Medicare Rights Center, explains: "Beneficiaries who don't plan ahead for deductibles, coinsurance, and premium surcharges can face unexpected and burdensome expenses." Understanding medicare deductibles and copays before a health crisis occurs, not during one, is essential financial planning.

Two primary solutions exist for closing this gap: Medigap plans and Medicare Advantage. Both provide a form of financial ceiling that Original Medicare alone does not offer.

Medicare Advantage vs. Medigap: Two Ways to Limit Your Out-of-Pocket Exposure

Medicare Advantage (Part C) plans are required by CMS to cap your out-of-pocket costs for covered in-network services. In 2026, the federal maximum is $9,250 though many plans set their limits lower. Once you hit that ceiling, the plan pays 100% of covered services for the rest of the year. As Mary Beth Donahue, President and CEO of AHIP, notes: "Medicare Advantage plans have become an important choice for beneficiaries seeking predictable out-of-pocket limits and additional benefits not available in Original Medicare."

However, Medicare Advantage out-of-pocket limits tell only part of the story. These plans operate within provider networks and may require prior authorization for certain services. Total cost of care, including what you pay to access the right doctors and treatments, matters more than the headline MOOP figure alone. Reviewing Original Medicare vs. Medicare Advantage differences carefully before enrolling can prevent surprises later.

Medigap plans work differently: they supplement Original Medicare by covering some or all of the cost-sharing gaps. Key figures for 2026:

Medigap Plan

Key Cost Feature in 2026

Plan G

Covers most cost-sharing after Part B deductible; most popular plan for new enrollees

Plan N

Covers most cost-sharing with small copays for office/ER visits

High-Deductible Plan G

$2,950 deductible before coverage begins; lower monthly premium

Plan K

$8,000 out-of-pocket maximum

Plan L

$4,000 out-of-pocket maximum

Choosing between Medicare Advantage and Medigap depends on your health needs, preferred doctors, travel patterns, and budget. Neither is universally better. A licensed Medicare agent can help you compare the total cost of care under each approach for your specific situation.

IRMAA and Higher-Income Medicare Costs: What to Know Before You Retire

If your income exceeds certain thresholds, you will pay more than the standard Medicare premium. The Income-Related Monthly Adjustment Amount (IRMAA) is a surcharge CMS adds to Part B and Part D premiums based on your 2024 Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI). Individual filers with income above $106,000 and joint filers above $212,000 are affected.

The 2026 Part B IRMAA tiers are:

Individual MAGI (2024)

Monthly Part B Premium (2026)

$106,000 or below

$202.90

$106,001 – $133,000

$284.10

$133,001 – $167,000

$405.80

$167,001 – $200,000

$527.50

$200,001 – $500,000

$649.20

Above $500,000

$689.90

Part D plans carry additional IRMAA surcharges of $14.50 to $91.00 per month on top of the plan premium. These amounts can significantly increase your total annual medicare cost sharing burden.

The good news: if your income has dropped since the look-back year due to a qualifying life-changing event, such as retirement, divorce, or the death of a spouse, you can appeal your IRMAA using Form SSA-44. Proactive strategies before retirement, like Roth IRA conversions to reduce future MAGI, can also help you avoid higher tiers altogether.

How to Reduce Your Medicare Out-of-Pocket Costs: Programs and Strategies That Help

Several programs and strategies can meaningfully lower what you pay. Knowing where to look, and acting during the right enrollment windows, makes a measurable difference.

Extra Help (Low-Income Subsidy): This federal program reduces or eliminates Part D costs for beneficiaries with limited income and resources. In 2025, the income limits are $23,475 for individuals and $31,725 for married couples (resource limits: $17,600 individual / $35,130 couple). Eligible beneficiaries pay little to nothing for covered prescriptions. Apply through SSA. gov or Medicare.

gov.

Medicare Savings Programs (MSPs): These state-run programs can cover Part B premiums, deductibles, and coinsurance for qualifying low-income beneficiaries. Contact your State Medical Assistance office or visit Medicare.gov to check eligibility. These programs can eliminate thousands of dollars in annual costs for those who qualify.

Additional strategies worth considering:

  • Review your plan every year during Annual Enrollment (October 15 – December 7) plans change their premiums, formularies, and cost-sharing annually

  • Use in-network providers when enrolled in Medicare Advantage to avoid higher out-of-network costs

  • Compare total cost of care not just premiums, when choosing between plans

  • Deduct qualifying medical expenses above 7.5% of AGI on your federal tax return; the 2026 standard deduction for those 65+ is $16,100 (single) or $32,200 (married filing jointly), plus an additional $2,050 for single filers 65+ and $1,650 per spouse for couples

Working with a licensed Medicare agent gives you access to side-by-side plan comparisons and personalized guidance on how to save money on Medicare supplement plans based on your specific health profile and budget.

Frequently Asked Questions About Medicare Out-of-Pocket Costs

What is the maximum out-of-pocket cost for Medicare in 2026?

Original Medicare has no out-of-pocket maximum. Medicare Advantage plans are capped at $9,250 for in-network covered services in 2026 (CMS), though many plans set their limits lower. Compare Medicare Advantage options during enrollment to find plans with a lower Medicare Advantage out-of-pocket maximum that fits your health needs and budget.

How much does Medicare Part B cost per month in 2026?

The standard Part B premium is $202.90 per month in 2026, plus a $283 annual deductible. Higher-income beneficiaries pay more due to IRMAA surcharges, with premiums reaching up to $689.90 per month. Most people have the premium deducted directly from their Social Security benefit each month.

Does Original Medicare have an out-of-pocket maximum?

No, Original Medicare does not have an annual out-of-pocket maximum, meaning costs can accumulate without a cap during a serious illness or extended hospital stay. Why Original Medicare lacks an out-of-pocket cap is a critical topic for anyone comparing coverage options. Medigap and Medicare Advantage are the two primary ways to add a financial ceiling.

What does Medicare not cover that I have to pay for out of pocket?

Original Medicare does not cover routine dental, vision, or hearing care, long-term custodial care, or most care received outside the United States, these are among the most common sources of unexpected original medicare expenses. Some Medicare Advantage plans include dental, vision, and hearing benefits, making total coverage comparison essential when choosing a plan.

How can I reduce my Medicare out-of-pocket costs?

Options include enrolling in a Medigap plan to cover gaps in Original Medicare, choosing a Medicare Advantage plan with a low out-of-pocket maximum, and applying for Extra Help or a Medicare Savings Program if you meet income and resource limits. Reviewing your plan annually during the October 15 – December 7 enrollment window can also prevent overpaying for coverage you are not fully using.

What is IRMAA and how does it affect my Medicare premiums?

IRMAA is an income-related surcharge added to your Part B and Part D premiums if your 2024 MAGI exceeded $106,000 (individual) or $212,000 (joint) it can add hundreds of dollars per month to your total Medicare cost. If your income has dropped due to a qualifying life-changing event, you can file an appeal with Social Security using Form SSA-44 to request a lower IRMAA tier.

What is the Medicare Part D catastrophic coverage threshold in 2026?

In 2026, once you spend $2,100 out of pocket on covered Part D prescriptions, you enter the catastrophic phase and pay $0 for covered drugs for the rest of the year. This cap, introduced under the Inflation Reduction Act, represents meaningful relief for beneficiaries managing expensive chronic conditions. Your plan's Explanation of Benefits will show where you stand relative to the catastrophic coverage threshold at any point during the year.

Managing Medicare out-of-pocket costs starts with knowing what you owe and why. The combination of Part A hospital costs, Part B coinsurance with no cap, Part D drug expenses, and potential IRMAA surcharges can add up faster than most people expect. The right supplemental coverage, whether Medigap or Medicare Advantage, paired with programs like Extra Help and Medicare Savings Programs, can dramatically reduce your financial exposure. If you want personalized help reviewing your options, speaking with a licensed Medicare agent is a practical next step. Visit

medicarefaq. com/faqs/medicare-maximum-out-of-pocket/">Medicare maximum out-of-pocket to explore your coverage options further.

author: David Haass
seoDescription: Learn about Medicare out-of-pocket costs including copays, deductibles, and coinsurance—plus tips to maximize savings and reduce what you pay for healthcare.
